I shall make myself a note as a matter of fact. Sure. I'll actually pass them all. Here's descriptive word finder. Here's the current biggest and best on steroids the czaris. Here's the words and more words. And what is your what are you telling me? Happy as a claim. It's lovely. If you look at number 26 on 191. There are really only four types of books, four types of writing for that matter. If you want big broad brush categories and so one is actual teaching. So some variation of a textbook in our field if you get Bob Stone's direct marketing you got yourself an honest-to-god textbook. It's not trying to convince you of anything. It's not trying to motivate you about anything. It's not trying to get you to particularly accept an idea one way or another. It's giving you the math and the procedures and the processes of a direct marketing business pretty much in plain vanilla form very much like an academic textbook. There are reference books meaning like these that are intended to be used again and again and again and again and again and again have no real purpose beyond that. The things that are most useful to us is story and advocacy and what's interesting is that again if you look at the the books like Thinking Grow Rich that won't die and won't go away they are constructed much like sales letters as opposed to book books and they are predominantly story books and advocacy books. They teach little or nothing and so for example Thinking Grow Rich the only real content in Thinking Grow Rich is the list of the 17 things that nobody here can remember the 17 principles right and and beyond that beyond that there's no particular help with actually implementing any of the 17 things so you really have a list of what to do which could be fit on one page you have no how to's. There's a couple of little tools not much there's a couple like there's a template for writing out goals and the rest of the entire book is a knitting together of stories designed to convince you of the wisdom of the 17 principles so it's a sales letter for the philosophy that's what it is and it is really constructed like a sales letter if you took the chapter breaks out and you stopped thinking about it as a book and you saw it has one long contiguous letter it wouldn't look much different from the sales letters that we write. Different publishers have made it look different by putting summary points at the end of each chapter you know that kind of stuff but if you took all that crap out and you put it together as a letter same thing's true of Malz. There's really only about three pages in the entire Psycho Cybernetics book that could even be generously called how to information. There's a there's about eight or ten pages if you condensed it of things you should be doing without how to support for how to do them but fundamentally it's a sales letter it's a persuasion document it's not a teaching document and you got to decide what you're setting out to do but you kind of got to be clear on it not get it in your own way and so generally speaking most of what we're doing we are trying to get them to accept something not necessarily learn it not necessarily know how to go do it but accept it and then we're trying to get them to do something next what is it that we want them to do next and so it's important to really get a grip when you sit down to write a book or a newsletter or whatever what it is you're attempting to do so somebody asked me on a break and we're going to get to the newsletter ultimately but see the first my first thing I'm working on is retention the second thing I'm working on is promotion actually teaching anything is pretty far down the damn list a they don't want to be taught anything and be it doesn't particularly serve our purposes principle number 27 page 192 the importance of the unimportant detail you can give two people the same joke and one of them can get laughs and the other one doesn't pretty much the three things that affect that by the way if you happen to want to get laughs with jokes one is the same thing that we were just talking about at lunch with Charlie and Bill is you know is is is the confidence of delivery you know if you tell a joke in a way that shows you expect it to be funny and you expect people to laugh they generally laugh if you tell it timidly or uncertainly but beyond that you know the the other two things on other is still is is is delivery is actual pace but then another is the detail you put into the joke and so oftentimes I can take a reader's digest joke or I can take a playboy joke cleaned up and I can make it usable for me and much funnier than it was in its printed form by adding a lot of little detail to it and in writing the little details make all the difference in the world the example I've given you here I mean I forget I don't know how many there are there because I didn't number them but here's a whole bunch of different ways that we might describe somebody in a blue shirt and every one of those descriptions tells you something different about the person but it's all about a blue shirt if you do a lot of it you eventually you're either kind of a good storyteller or you're not a good storyteller and the more of it you do the better at it you get and eventually you're doing this stuff kind of unconsciously but until you're conscious you got to take the one you wrote and now go back and look at it for detail and so you there's not much you want in a story that is like left alone like he was wearing a blue shirt you got to kind of immediately say to yourself well shit we can do better than that you know what are we trying to convey here what can we say about this person this place this thing this event that that that makes it more meaningful the further away you are from just telling the story the better off that you are I mean there's very few things that are inherently persuasive or inherently funny there's a few things that are inherently funny but there's very few things that are inherently funny moose are inherently more funny than deer by the way any comedian I'll tell you that by the way if you're much better off with a moose because for some reason people think mooses are funny and so they start out thinking the story is gonna be fine but there's very few things that are inherently funny and there's very few things that are inherently persuasive so all this little detail makes a difference principle 28 we've kind of already talked about this I mean the ultimately we're not selling things we're not we know not to sell features we're not just selling benefits we're really selling on a basis of values and ultimately we're selling people what they want so we're really selling aspirations so people buy because of symbolism so you know
